title: Translating regulatory clauses into executable codes for building design checking via large language model driven function matching and composing
contribution: 首次将LLM应用于复杂建筑设计条款的自动化解释与可执行代码生成，提出原子函数库和基于规则的自适应提示工程方法LLM-FuncMapper
markdownFile: Zheng, Z. etal - 2026 - Translating regulatory clauses into executable codes for building design checking via large language....md
pdfFile: Zheng, Z. etal - 2026 - Translating regulatory clauses into executable codes for building design checking via large language....pdf
abstract: Translating clauses into executable code is a vital stage of automated rule checking (ARC) and is essential for effective building design compliance checking, particularly for rules with implicit properties or complex logic requiring domain knowledge. Thus, by systematically analyzing building clauses, 66 atomic functions are defined first to encapsulate common computational logics. Then, LLM-FuncMapper is proposed, a large language model (LLM)-based approach with rule-based adaptive prompts that match clauses to atomic functions. Finally, executable code is generated by composing functions through the LLMs. Experiments show LLM-FuncMapper outperforms fine-tuning methods by 19% in function matching while significantly reducing manual annotation efforts. Case study demonstrates that LLM-FuncMapper can automatically compose multiple atomic functions to generate executable code, boosting rule-checking efficiency. To our knowledge, this research represents the first application of LLMs for interpreting complex design clauses into executable code, which may shed light on further adoption of LLMs in the construction domain.
methodology: The proposed LLM-FuncMapper comprises two main components: (1) An atomic function library of 66 functions derived from systematic analysis of Chapters 3-5 of the Chinese fire protection code (GB 50016-2014), categorized into eight types (existence, quantity, geometry, distance, area, space_location, wall-window ratio, property) with 5 low-order and 61 high-order functions capturing complex computational logics including topological, geometric, and distance calculations. (2) Rule-based adaptive prompt engineering for LLM-based function matching, incorporating a prompt template with four parts (role, library, example with CoT reasoning, analysis section) and a keyword-based inference strategy to filter irrelevant functions and shorten prompts. The method uses few-shot prompting with chain-of-thought to enable LLMs to identify key phrases, infer function categories and object categories, and recommend up to 5 atomic functions per clause. Validation includes statistical analysis of the function library, experiments comparing LLMs against fine-tuned BERT on 410 annotated sentences using Recall@k metrics, and a proof-of-concept rule checking on an actual plant building using IFC models and IFCOpenShell.
results: Claude 3.5-Sonnet with rule-based adaptive prompt achieved the highest Recall@5avg of 81.55%, surpassing the fine-tuned BERT model (68.50%) by 19%. The rule-based adaptive prompt improved Recall@5avg by 4.6%, 5.6%, 1.2%, and 2.8% for ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.5-Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3, and DeepSeek-R1 respectively over full-prompt baselines. DeepSeek-R1 had the lowest hallucination rate (0.14%), followed by Claude 3.5-Sonnet (0.29%). Nearly perfect F1 scores were achieved on simple clauses and complex clauses with a single atomic function; for complex clauses with 2-3 atomic functions, F1 consistently exceeded 0.8. The proof-of-concept demonstrated automatic generation of executable Python code from complex regulatory clauses, with LLM-FuncMapper producing function recommendations in approximately 5 seconds per clause versus several minutes for manual expert analysis.
